title: What being an AI Researcher actually involves
summary:
AI research is not about prompting chatbots or following tutorial notebooks. It is about reading dense mathematical papers, implementing algorithms from scratch, running experiments that fail far more often than they succeed, and slowly building intuition for why certain approaches work and others do not. Most of your time is spent staring at loss curves, debugging tensor shapes, and trying to reproduce results from papers that left out critical details.

The work sits at the intersection of mathematics, software engineering, and scientific method. You need strong linear algebra and probability foundations, fluency in at least one deep learning framework, and the patience to iterate on ideas for weeks or months before seeing meaningful results. Writing is a core skill — your papers need to clearly communicate what you tried, what worked, and what the community should take away.

What makes this field unusual is the speed at which the frontier moves. A technique that was state-of-the-art six months ago may already be obsolete. You are constantly learning, constantly reading, and constantly adapting your mental models. The researchers who thrive are not just technically strong — they are deeply curious, comfortable with ambiguity, and willing to pursue ideas that might not pan out.
coreWork: Reading and critically evaluating research papers (3-5 per week minimum)
Designing and running experiments on GPU clusters
Debugging training runs — diagnosing loss spikes, gradient issues, and convergence failures
Writing research code in PyTorch, JAX, or TensorFlow
Iterating on model architectures and training procedures
Analysing experimental results with statistical rigour
Writing papers and preparing conference submissions
Collaborating with other researchers through code reviews and paper discussions
Managing compute budgets and optimising GPU utilisation
frontierPull: You are building intelligence itself. Every other field uses AI as a tool — you are the one making the tool. The problems are among the hardest in science, the pace of progress is extraordinary, and the work has direct implications for how billions of people will live and work. Few fields offer this combination of intellectual depth, practical impact, and sheer novelty.
